briefing.headline: 1 high-priority item in the latest EM scan
briefing.summary: Top sources today: St Emlyn's, REBEL EM, Journal of Emergency Medicine. Main themes: trials, critical care crossover, reviews. Best early reads: To squeeze or not to squeeze. The ARISE-FLUIDS trial.
briefing.editorialHeadline: Fluid Wars, Oxygen Titration, and PE Triage: Key Shifts in Critical Care Practice
briefing.editorialSummary:
The resuscitation debate continues to yield caution; the ARISE-FLUIDS data suggest that whether you favor early vasopressors with restricted fluids or liberal initial fluid boluses, neither strategy demonstrated a clear advantage for 90-day survival in septic shock. This lack of definitive superiority should temper assumptions about aggressive fluid management at the bedside.

Meanwhile, optimizing oxygenation requires vigilance: while implementing formal guidelines to curb severe hyperoxemia is beneficial, one must be acutely aware that this can inadvertently shift the balance toward inadequate oxygen delivery. Furthermore, when managing low-risk acute PE in the DOAC era, evidence suggests hospitalization may not confer a survival benefit over outpatient management for sPESI 0 or 1 patients.

Beyond shock and oxygenation, consider the procedural details: When performing RSI, remember that regardless of whether you use rocuronium or succinylcholine, proactively planning for adequate post-intubation analgesia is paramount. Finally, in pediatric OHCA, elevated lactate measured within six hours of ROSC warrants consideration as a potential biomarker for poorer long-term outcomes.

dailyEditorial.title: To squeeze or not to squeeze. The ARISE-FLUIDS trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: The ARISE-FLUIDS data suggest caution when titrating between early vasopressor initiation with restricted fluids versus liberal initial fluid resuscitation. Since the trial did not show improved 90-day survival with either strategy, clinicians should approach these protocols without assuming a definitive benefit to one over the other at the bedside.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review discusses the ARISE-FLUIDS trial, which directly compared two distinct resuscitation strategies in septic shock patients presenting to the emergency department: one group received early vasopressors alongsid…
dailyEditorial.summary: This review discusses the ARISE-FLUIDS trial, which directly compared two distinct resuscitation strategies in septic shock patients presenting to the emergency department: one group received early vasopressors alongside restricted fluid administration, while the other received higher fluid volumes with later initiation of vasopressors. The primary finding reported is that neither of these approaches demonstrated superiority over the other regarding the rate of survival at day 90. This lack of difference suggests that current resuscitation protocols involving aggressive fluid management versus early pressor use may not yield a clear advantage in terms of long-term outcomes for this critically ill population.

dailyEditorial.title: Rocuronium vs Succinylcholine for RSI: Awareness, Paralysis, and Post-Intubation Sedation
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When choosing a paralytic for RSI, remember that while rocuronium might delay starting post-intubation sedation compared to succinylcholine, the absolute difference is small. Regardless of the agent used, always plan ahead for adequate analgesia and sedation *before* intubating to mitigate the risk of intraoperative awareness.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review discusses a retrospective study comparing the use of rocuronium versus succinylcholine during Rapid Sequence Intubation (RSI) in the emergency department setting, focusing on post-intubation sedation and ana…
dailyEditorial.summary: This review discusses a retrospective study comparing the use of rocuronium versus succinylcholine during Rapid Sequence Intubation (RSI) in the emergency department setting, focusing on post-intubation sedation and analgesia needs. The core finding suggests that while rocuronium was linked to delayed initiation of post-intubation sedation compared to succinylcholine, the magnitude of this difference appeared small, leading to uncertainty regarding its true clinical significance. A key concern raised is the potential for awareness during paralysis when utilizing longer-acting agents like rocuronium. Consequently, the authors recommend proactively planning for adequate post-intubation sedation and analgesia before proceeding with RSI, particularly when using drugs with prolonged effects.

dailyEditorial.title: Reducing Hyperoxemia in Mechanically Ventilated Emergency Department Patients: A Before-and-After Study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When implementing oxygen titration protocols, be mindful that reducing hyperoxia might increase the risk of developing hypoxemia. Don't assume a guideline will optimize oxygenation without careful monitoring; maintaining appropriate FiO2 and PEEP remains critical. Further research is needed to establish safe, optimal targets for all ventilation parameters.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This before-and-after study assessed the impact of implementing a formal oxygen titration guideline in the emergency department setting for mechanically ventilated patients.
dailyEditorial.summary: This before-and-after study assessed the impact of implementing a formal oxygen titration guideline in the emergency department setting for mechanically ventilated patients. The authors found that adopting this structured approach successfully reduced the incidence of severe hyperoxemia among the cohort. However, this benefit came at a cost, as the implementation was associated with an increase in overall hypoxemia. Notably, the rate of severe hypoxemia did not change following guideline adoption. Overall, the findings suggest that while guidelines can effectively curb excessive oxygen delivery, they may inadvertently shift the balance toward inadequate oxygenation.

dailyEditorial.title: Maintenance of prehospital anaesthesia using an intermittent bolus regime in blunt trauma patients with a high GCS and hemodynamic reserve: a retrospective cohort study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When maintaining anesthesia prehospital in stable blunt trauma patients (GCS ≥ 9), be mindful that intermittent boluses alone can lead to highly variable cumulative drug exposure. Consider supplementing or adjusting your technique if you suspect dosing variability might compromise anesthetic depth, as relying solely on this method risks sub-therapeutic levels. This suggests a need for more standardized infusion protocols over purely bolus-based maintenance.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This retrospective cohort study examined the practice of maintaining anesthesia in prehospital blunt trauma patients who were hemodynamically stable and had a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) of 9 or greater, specifically focus…
dailyEditorial.summary: This retrospective cohort study examined the practice of maintaining anesthesia in prehospital blunt trauma patients who were hemodynamically stable and had a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) of 9 or greater, specifically focusing on those managed with an intermittent bolus-only regimen. The authors concluded that relying solely on intermittent boluses for anesthetic maintenance introduces significant variability into the total cumulative doses administered to these patients. This inherent dosing unpredictability raises a genuine concern regarding the potential for plasma levels to fall below therapeutic thresholds in the field setting. While the cohort was relatively homogeneous, the core finding points toward an issue with dose consistency when using this specific maintenance strategy.

dailyEditorial.title: A multi-center retrospective cohort study of SUGAmmadex for neuromuscular blockade reversal in the emergency department: SUGARED study - on behalf of EMPHARM-NET Investigators
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Be mindful that rocuronium reversal with sugammadex in TBI/sICH patients isn't just about muscle tone; monitor closely for changes in GCS and analgesic requirements post-reversal. Given the association found, consider reassessing sedation needs rather than assuming a stable baseline state immediately after block reversal.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This multi-center retrospective cohort study, the SUGARED study, examined the use of sugammadex to reverse rocuronium blockade specifically within the emergency department setting for patients who had sustained traumati…
dailyEditorial.summary: This multi-center retrospective cohort study, the SUGARED study, examined the use of sugammadex to reverse rocuronium blockade specifically within the emergency department setting for patients who had sustained traumatic brain injury or intracranial hemorrhage. The authors found a notable association between administering sugammadex and subsequent changes in both Glasgow Coma Scale scores and the need for analgesia/sedation. This suggests that simply reversing the neuromuscular block might have downstream effects on neurological status or pain management requirements beyond just muscle function. While the finding is interesting, it is important to remember this was a retrospective analysis.

dailyEditorial.title: Critical Care Evidence Updates – May 2026
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For CVAD management, keep the evidence regarding 4% Tetrasodium EDTA for infection prophylaxis in mind, but remember this is part of a broader discussion. When managing male ICU patients with urinary catheters, consider implementing securement bands proactively to mitigate meatal pressure injury risk. These updates suggest incorporating these specific, low-cost interventions into routine rounds.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This month's critical care update synthesizes several recent findings, touching on device-related complications, delirium management, and basic patient care measures.
dailyEditorial.summary: This month's critical care update synthesizes several recent findings, touching on device-related complications, delirium management, and basic patient care measures. Notably, there is discussion around the use of 4% Tetrasodium EDTA for preventing central venous access device (CVAD) site infections, alongside reviewing nurse-led protocols for delirium prevention which suggests a role for non-physician staff in these areas. For routine bedside practice, the literature specifically draws attention to using urinary catheter securement bands as a measure to prevent meatal pressure injuries in male intensive care unit patients. Overall, it provides a broad overview of evolving best practices across several core critical care domains.

dailyEditorial.title: Association of Serum Lactate with Outcome After Pediatric Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest: A Secondary Analysis of the Therapeutic Hypothermia After Pediatric Cardiac Arrest (THAPCA) Trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Elevated lactate measured within 6 hours of ROSC should prompt consideration of poorer long-term prognosis following pediatric OHCA. This suggests that serial lactate monitoring could be useful for risk stratification, but clinicians should interpret this alongside established neurological and physiological scores, as the utility is currently suggested rather than definitively proven.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This secondary analysis re-examined the THAPCA-OH trial data to specifically look at serum lactate levels as a predictor of long-term outcomes following pediatric out-of-hospital cardiac arrest.
dailyEditorial.summary: This secondary analysis re-examined the THAPCA-OH trial data to specifically look at serum lactate levels as a predictor of long-term outcomes following pediatric out-of-hospital cardiac arrest. The key finding reported is that elevated lactate concentrations measured within the first six hours after return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) correlated with poorer one-year survival and unfavorable neurological outcomes. This suggests that lactate may serve as an adjunct biomarker for early risk stratification in this critically ill population. While promising, it's important to remember this is a secondary analysis drawing conclusions about prognostic utility from existing trial data.

dailyEditorial.title: Critical care delivery models in emergency departments: a systematic review of the literature and meta-analysis of related outcome effects
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If your facility is considering optimizing its approach to managing critically ill patients in the ED, these findings suggest that establishing a dedicated critical care zone, like an ED-ICU, warrants consideration. This model appears linked to better outcomes, specifically lower rates of both ICU transfer and overall hospital stay duration compared to less structured models. Remember this is based on meta-analysis, so local resource availability must guide implementation.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesized the literature on various organizational models for delivering critical care within the emergency department setting, culminating in a meta-analysis of related outcomes.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review synthesized the literature on various organizational models for delivering critical care within the emergency department setting, culminating in a meta-analysis of related outcomes. The authors categorized several approaches, with dedicated critical care areas, such as an ED-ICU setup, being the most frequently documented model. The quantitative analysis performed suggests a tangible benefit to implementing these specialized zones. Specifically, compared to other established CC-ED delivery models, dedicating space and resources within the ED for critical care appears associated with reductions in both subsequent ICU admission rates and overall hospital length of stay for critically ill patients presenting through the department.

dailyEditorial.title: PulmCrit: Is oseltamivir assassinating ICU patients?
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Given the preliminary data suggesting a high likelihood of increased mortality with oseltamivir in critically ill patients, exercise extreme caution before administering it. While further review is necessary, these findings strongly suggest that current guidelines may need re-evaluation for this drug class in the ICU setting. Do not change established protocols without reviewing the full dataset and institutional consensus.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The REMAP-CAP adaptive platform trial has released preliminary data regarding the use of oseltamivir in critically ill patients, and the findings are quite alarming.
dailyEditorial.summary: The REMAP-CAP adaptive platform trial has released preliminary data regarding the use of oseltamivir in critically ill patients, and the findings are quite alarming. The primary analysis presented suggests a substantial association between oseltamivir administration and increased mortality risk in this vulnerable population. Specifically, the reported likelihood of causing increased mortality was calculated at 98.3%. Given these preliminary results, it warrants immediate attention for any current protocols involving antiviral prophylaxis or treatment with oseltamivir in the ICU setting. Clinicians should treat this data with caution but recognize its potential impact on practice.

dailyEditorial.title: Infant with seizure
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When evaluating an infant with concerning signs after trauma, remember that bedside cranial ultrasound can be instrumental in localizing intracranial collections. Finding multilayered fluid collections with associated vascular changes strongly favors a subdural collection over SAH or hygromas. However, always correlate these imaging findings with the clinical picture and consider the limitations of ultrasound in this age group.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This case report details the management of a 5-month-old infant presenting with concerning neurological signs, including irritability and tonic posturing following a minor fall.
dailyEditorial.summary: This case report details the management of a 5-month-old infant presenting with concerning neurological signs, including irritability and tonic posturing following a minor fall. Key physical exam findings included ecchymoses and a tense anterior fontanelle. The crucial diagnostic step involved bedside cranial ultrasonography, which visualized bilateral heterogeneous, multilayered collections. These ultrasound findings were interpreted by the authors as highly suggestive of subdural hematomas (SDH), effectively differentiating them from other potential diagnoses like subarachnoid hemorrhage or hygromas. The use of color Doppler to show vessel displacement away from the cortical surface further supported the diagnosis of SDH.

dailyEditorial.title: ACMT Toxicology Visual Pearl – Black Vomit, Big Problem
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When a patient presents with black emesis and a garlicky or fishy odor, immediately consider ingestion of zinc or aluminum phosphide rodenticides. Be hypervigilant for signs of impending cardiovascular collapse, as profound shock can develop rapidly despite an initially stable appearance. Supportive care focusing on airway management and circulatory support is paramount until definitive toxicology workup can be completed.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This visual pearl highlights the critical toxidrome associated with ingesting zinc or aluminum phosphide rodenticides, which are often encountered as black pellets.
dailyEditorial.summary: This visual pearl highlights the critical toxidrome associated with ingesting zinc or aluminum phosphide rodenticides, which are often encountered as black pellets. Ingestion leads to a rapid onset of symptoms characterized by nausea and vomiting dark emesis that has a distinct garlicky or fishy odor. The underlying mechanism involves the release of phosphine gas, which is highly toxic because it inhibits mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation, leading to significant cardiovascular compromise. Clinically, this presentation can be deceptive; patients may appear stable initially but are at high risk for rapid decompensation into profound shock. Recognizing the source and the associated pathophysiology is key to early management.

dailyEditorial.title: ECG Cases 62 – ACLS Arrhythmia Pitfalls, Part 5: Stable Narrow Complex Tachycardias
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When faced with a stable narrow complex tachycardia, don't rely solely on rate or regular rhythm; systematically evaluate for atrial flutter versus true SVT, paying close attention to any subtle waveform changes. Always maintain a high index of suspicion for secondary causes like electrolyte abnormalities or drug effects before committing to cardioversion or rate control agents.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This installment of the ECG Cases series tackles the common but often tricky pitfalls encountered when interpreting stable narrow complex tachycardias in the emergency setting.
dailyEditorial.summary: This installment of the ECG Cases series tackles the common but often tricky pitfalls encountered when interpreting stable narrow complex tachycardias in the emergency setting. The resource uses eight real-world case examples to walk through the nuances required to accurately differentiate between sinus tachycardia, atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, and supraventricular tachycardia (SVT). It's a valuable refresher because these rhythms can look deceptively similar on an ECG, and misdiagnosis can lead to inappropriate or harmful management. The emphasis here is not just on pattern recognition but also on considering the underlying secondary causes that might be driving the rhythm and guiding definitive treatment decisions.

dailyEditorial.title: Predicting Echocardiography Findings in Adults Presenting to the Emergency Department With Syncope: An External Validation of the ROMEO Score
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For stable patients presenting with syncope, consider using the ROMEO score to stratify TTE necessity; a zero score suggests a very low likelihood of significant cardiac findings. This could help streamline resource use by safely withholding an echo in appropriately low-risk individuals. Remember that this is a predictive tool, and clinical judgment must always guide the decision, especially if the patient presentation remains atypical.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This paper presents an external validation of the ROMEO score, a tool designed to predict whether a patient presenting with syncope will have significant findings on a Transthoracic Echocardiogram (TTE).
dailyEditorial.summary: This paper presents an external validation of the ROMEO score, a tool designed to predict whether a patient presenting with syncope will have significant findings on a Transthoracic Echocardiogram (TTE). The core finding is that a zero ROMEO score exhibits remarkably strong predictive performance, boasting a 98.6% Negative Predictive Value. This suggests that patients scoring zero are highly unlikely to benefit from the diagnostic yield of TTE. Therefore, the authors argue this score can serve as a useful triage mechanism in the busy emergency department setting. It allows clinicians to potentially de-escalate care by avoiding unnecessary echo testing for low-risk syncope presentations.

dailyEditorial.title: Acute urinary retention presenting as inferior pseudo-STEMI: A case report
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When you see isolated, reversible inferior ST-segment elevation without clear cardiac etiology or hemodynamic instability, strongly consider massive urinary retention. Decompression is often curative and simple; however, remember that even if the ECG normalizes quickly, a thorough workup for true ischemia should still be completed.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This case report details a scenario where massive bladder distension presented on ECG mimicking an inferior STEMI, which is a classic but potentially missed diagnosis in the ED.
dailyEditorial.summary: This case report details a scenario where massive bladder distension presented on ECG mimicking an inferior STEMI, which is a classic but potentially missed diagnosis in the ED. The authors highlight that simple decompression with a Foley catheter was sufficient to resolve the ST-segment elevation completely, even in the absence of typical signs of hemodynamic compromise such as tachycardia or hypertension. This underscores that profound urinary retention can cause significant, reversible electrical changes mimicking acute coronary syndrome findings. It serves as an excellent reminder for all emergency physicians to keep this diagnosis high on the differential list when encountering unexplained inferior ST-elevation.

dailyEditorial.title: Outcomes of Acute PE Treated With DOACs in the Veterans Affairs Health System: A Retrospective Cohort Study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For your low-risk sPESI patients (0 or 1), you can feel more confident discharging them rather than admitting them solely for acute PE management, as hospitalization didn't improve 30-day mortality. Remember that this finding is specific to the DOAC era and does not negate the need for appropriate anticoagulation initiation. Always ensure there isn't another compelling reason requiring admission.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This retrospective cohort study analyzed the 30-day mortality outcomes in a large U.S.
dailyEditorial.summary: This retrospective cohort study analyzed the 30-day mortality outcomes in a large U.S. population presenting with acute pulmonary embolism (PE) who were managed with direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs). The authors stratified their analysis based on the Wells' criteria equivalent, specifically using the sPESI score. The key finding was that for patients classified as low risk, meaning those with an sPESI of 0 or 1, receiving inpatient hospitalization did not appear to confer a survival benefit compared to being managed in the outpatient setting. This suggests that current practice may be over-hospitalizing a significant portion of low-risk PE patients.

dailyEditorial.title: No Pause, No Problem? Using Doppler Ultrasound to Detect ROSC Without Pausing Compressions for Pulse Check
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If you are concerned about pulse checks compromising CPR quality, remember that Doppler assessment of femoral artery waveforms during compressions may offer a way to screen for ROSC without pausing chest compressions. While this concept is promising for workflow improvement, its utility should be viewed as an adjunct tool rather than a replacement for established protocols; always maintain high-quality compressions until definitive rhythm confirmation.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review tackles the persistent issue of interruptions during CPR, specifically focusing on how routine pulse checks disrupt high-quality chest compressions.
dailyEditorial.summary: This review tackles the persistent issue of interruptions during CPR, specifically focusing on how routine pulse checks disrupt high-quality chest compressions. The core concept explored is leveraging continuous femoral arterial Doppler ultrasound waveforms obtained *during* active compressions to detect signs of Return of Spontaneous Circulation (ROSC). Instead of pausing compressions for a traditional palpation or line check, the authors evaluate if detecting pulsatility and anterograde flow signals provides an accurate surrogate marker for true cardiac activity. This approach suggests a potential paradigm shift in resuscitation protocols by allowing continuous monitoring without sacrificing compression quality.

dailyEditorial.title: Status epilepticus in patients with known epilepsy presenting to the emergency department: Predictors and mortality risk
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Given the high incidence and associated mortality risk, consider proactively stratifying epilepsy patients presenting with altered mental status. Pay extra attention to those who are elderly, have known metabolic issues, or whose presentation suggests an underlying CNS source for potential SE. This approach can guide timely escalation of care.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This study provides a local perspective on the incidence and predictors of status epilepticus (SE) in epilepsy patients presenting to the emergency department, noting that SE was quite common, affecting nearly one-quart…
dailyEditorial.summary: This study provides a local perspective on the incidence and predictors of status epilepticus (SE) in epilepsy patients presenting to the emergency department, noting that SE was quite common, affecting nearly one-quarter of this cohort. Crucially, the authors highlight a strong association between developing SE and increased in-hospital mortality. The findings suggest that risk stratification is warranted for these patients upon presentation. Specifically, they point toward older age, metabolic derangements, or suspicion of central nervous system infection as key areas to focus on during initial assessment.

dailyEditorial.title: Risk of obstructive acute kidney injury: derivation and internal validation of a risk stratification tree
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For a patient presenting with AKI where urological etiology is suspected, consider using this risk stratification tool if available; acute flank or hypogastric pain strongly elevates suspicion for obstruction. Remember that the model's utility is currently limited to internal validation, so do not rely on it as definitive proof at the bedside. Regardless of the calculated risk score, a thorough physical exam and appropriate imaging remain paramount.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This paper introduces the KIT-FISTO model, a newly developed risk stratification decision tree designed to predict obstructive acute kidney injury (AKI) in the emergency department setting.
dailyEditorial.summary: This paper introduces the KIT-FISTO model, a newly developed risk stratification decision tree designed to predict obstructive acute kidney injury (AKI) in the emergency department setting. The study utilized a retrospective derivation and internal validation cohort approach on adult patients presenting with AKI of any KDIGO stage. The model stratifies risk based on clinical presentation; specifically, patients reporting lumbar, flank, or hypogastric pain are categorized as 'high risk,' showing an associated obstructive AKI risk around 55%. A 'moderate risk' group is defined for those lacking acute pain but possessing specific histories such as prior urinary tract surgery or abdominal cancer. The authors note that the model achieved high sensitivity in identifying low-risk patients, though they caution that external and prospective validation is necessary before clinical implementation.

dailyEditorial.title: Airway Foreign Bodies
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When considering an airway foreign body, remember to maintain a high level of suspicion across age groups, especially in the very young and elderly. For initial management in adults, flexible bronchoscopy is the preferred first step unless you suspect a large or deeply impacted object requiring rigid instrumentation. Always consider the potential for subtle presentations that might otherwise be missed.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This overview provides a comprehensive look at airway foreign bodies (AFBs), emphasizing that these can be potentially life-threatening emergencies.
dailyEditorial.summary: This overview provides a comprehensive look at airway foreign bodies (AFBs), emphasizing that these can be potentially life-threatening emergencies. A key point highlighted is the demographic predilection, noting that while they affect various groups, suspicion should remain high in both children under five and adults over sixty-five. The article details the pathophysiology, potential complications, and diagnostic workup for these objects. Management strategies are differentiated based on object size and location; flexible bronchoscopy is generally recommended as the initial approach for most adult cases, whereas rigid bronchoscopy retains its critical role for larger or more proximal impactions. Given that presentations can be subtle, maintaining a high index of suspicion remains paramount.

dailyEditorial.title: Real-time identification of aetiology in patients able to undergo transoesophageal echocardiography with non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest in China: a prospective, single-centre exploratory study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: TEE appears to be a rapid and feasible tool for identifying potential causes during CPR in NT-OHCA patients, with acute aortic dissection being a notable finding here. However, remember this is an exploratory series lacking a control arm; therefore, do not change your standard resuscitation algorithm based on this data alone. Use it as supportive information while awaiting definitive diagnostic confirmation.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This single-center study explored the utility of transoesophageal echocardiography (TEE) for identifying reversible causes in patients presenting with non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (NT-OHCA) in China.
dailyEditorial.summary: This single-center study explored the utility of transoesophageal echocardiography (TEE) for identifying reversible causes in patients presenting with non-traumatic out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (NT-OHCA) in China. The authors found that TEE successfully provided diagnostic images in all 43 eligible patients who underwent the procedure, suggesting its feasibility during active resuscitation efforts. Reversible etiologies were identified in 39% of cases analyzed, with acute aortic dissection being the most frequently diagnosed condition at 50%. While TEE guided interventions occurred in over half the identified cases, the study noted that there was no significant difference in return of spontaneous circulation or 28-day survival rates between those whose cause was identified versus those whose cause remained unknown. Crucially, the authors emphasized that due to the lack of a comparator group, these findings warrant further controlled investigation.
